reason: HTML is the markup language used to build web pages; Pascal/Assembly/C are general-purpose, not web page languages.

TCP/IP is a protocol suite, not a topology; Token Ring/Ethernet/ATM are at least associated with physical layouts (ring/bus-star/star), so TCP/IP is the clear odd one out. (Question is loose: Ethernet is strictly a technology too.)
An extranet is an intranet extended to authorized external parties.
Plug and play = device auto-detection/configuration so the system works with minimal user technical effort.
Standard IT-literacy convention: a major upgrade is a new version, a minor upgrade is a release.
PowerPoint is the presentation application; Access = database, Project = project mgmt, Java = language.

Bridges segment a LAN into more, smaller collision domains, thereby reducing collisions. Stem asks for TWO purposes but the second valid option ('add collision domains') is absent; B/C/D are all false (bridges do not create broadcast domains), so only A stands. Flawed stem.
Telnet is a remote-login protocol used to reach multi-user hosts/servers; both A and B describe it.
Tape is sequential-access and far slower than random-access disk; the times are not the same. False.
Currently executing OS, programs and their data reside in main memory (RAM).
Full backup copies all files to tape/disk; A describes differential, C describes incremental.
Silberschatz's textbook definition: a collection of processors that share neither memory nor a clock, each with local memory, communicating over a network. B/C are vague paraphrases.

Memory is a primary component, so the complete list (I/O + processor + memory + auxiliary storage) is B. C is only right under the legacy Shelly-Cashman convention where 'processor unit' is defined to contain CPU + main memory. Genuinely ambiguous - needs human adjudication.

DNS Server is a Server-only role; Win2000 Professional already supports 2 CPUs (so B is no differentiator) and already ships IIS 5.0 sufficient for a small web server (so C is a weak reason). Garbled, recall-based stem.
Standardized Ethernet (10BASE5, IEEE 802.3) ran at 10 Mbps; 100 Mbps and 1 Mbps StarLAN came later.
DDS = Digital Data Service (digital); DDD (Direct Distance Dialing) and WATS are analog telephone services; DDT is not a comms service.
Prim's algorithm computes a minimum spanning tree; Huffman = coding, Dijkstra = shortest path, DFS = traversal.

Dijkstra is the canonical single-source shortest-path algorithm. Note the item is flawed: Bellman-Ford (A) also solves shortest path (and handles negative weights), so two options are technically correct.
int *p; declares a pointer; &p is a reference, plain int p is a value, Ptr is not a type. (Capitalized 'Int' is an OCR/typo artifact.)
D flip-flop next state equals the D input regardless of present state; figure at /figures/knpc2016-q007.png shows exactly this table.
